title: Matthew 5
subtitle: The Beatitudes; Salt and Light; The Law Fulfilled; The Antitheses

heb.paragraph: Not 'happy' in the modern emotional sense but 'favoured by God.' Each Beatitude declares that people the world considers unfortunate — the poor, the mourning, the meek — are in fact in an enviable position because God's kingdom belongs to them. The word reverses social evaluation.

'Poor in spirit' uses ptōchos (abjectly poor, beggars) rather than penēs (working poor). These are not people with modest means but people with nothing — spiritually bankrupt, utterly dependent. The kingdom belongs first to those who know they have nothing to offer.

Not weakness but strength under control — a war horse trained to obey, power submitted to purpose. Jesus applies the word to himself (11:29). The meek 'inherit the earth' (quoting Ps 37:11), not through conquest but through patient trust in God's justice.

hist.context: The Sermon on the Mount (chs.5–7) is the first of Matthew's five discourses — the new Torah delivered by the new Moses from a mountain (echoing Sinai). The Beatitudes function as the preamble to the sermon: they describe the character of the community Jesus is forming, not a programme for earning kingdom status. The eight Beatitudes are arranged chiastically, with the first and eighth sharing the same promise (kingdom of heaven), and the fourth (hunger and thirst for righteousness) at the centre.

refs.note: The Spirit of the Lord is on me... to proclaim good news to the poor, to bind up the broken-hearted. The Beatitudes echo Isaiah's Servant mission — the poor, the mourning, the meek are the recipients of God's kingdom.
The meek will inherit the earth — the exact language of Beatitude 3. Jesus is citing the Psalter as the portrait of kingdom character.
The Seven Woes are the exact inversion of the Eight Beatitudes — blessed/woe, kingdom/condemnation.

notes.note: Calvin: The Beatitudes describe those who are broken before God and in the world. The world counts the poor in spirit unfortunate; Christ calls them blessed. These Beatitudes reverse every worldly valuation of blessedness — they describe the character that is formed by the kingdom's arrival, not the effort that produces admission to it.

Calvin on the first Antithesis: Christ goes beyond the letter to the spirit. The law says do not murder; Christ says do not hate. He does not contradict the law but extends it to its full demand — what the law forbade externally, Christ forbids internally. He is not adding to the law; he is uncovering what it always meant.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the Beatitudes discusses makarios and its relationship to Hebrew ashre (the happiness of the righteous person in Pss 1:1; 32:1). Each Beatitude describes a present character and promises a future reality — the kingdom belongs to the poor in spirit (present) and they shall see God (future).

The NET note on plēroō traces the term through Matthew (1:22; 2:15, 17, 23; 3:15; 4:14) — consistently used for the completion/realisation of OT anticipation. Jesus understands his life as the filling-full of everything the OT pointed toward, including the law's deepest demands.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes that makarios (blessed) in classical Greek described the happy state of the gods — untouched by mortal cares. The Beatitudes apply this word to the poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek — the opposite of the world's candidates for divine happiness. He identifies eight Beatitudes (vv.11-12 expand v.10) describing the character of kingdom citizens, not the conditions for entering.

Robertson observes that 'fulfil' (plērōsai) means to bring to complete expression — Jesus does not cancel the law but brings out its full meaning and demand. The Pharisees' righteousness is inadequate not because it is too rigorous but because it is not rigorous enough at the level of the heart.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine on the Beatitudes: The poor in spirit are those not puffed up with vain pride. The mourners grieve for sins — their own or others. The meek do not resist injuries but overcome evil with good. The hungry for righteousness love what is just for its own sake. Each Beatitude describes a different dimension of the same life of humility before God.

Chrysostom: He says he came to fulfil the law — not to add to it as if deficient, but to complete it as if unfinished. The law was a seed; the gospel is the fruit. The law pointed forward; the gospel is the arrival of what it pointed to. Not one iota shall pass — the moral law is permanent under its gospel fulfilment.

sections.header: Verses 13–20 — Salt, Light, and the Fulfilment of the Law

heb.paragraph: Jesus did not come to abolish (katalyō) the Law but to fulfil (plēroō) it. The verb means to bring to full expression, to fill to the brim. Jesus is not adding rules but revealing what the Law always meant — its deepest intention, not just its surface requirements.

A key Matthean word (7 times). The disciples' righteousness must 'surpass' (perisseuō — overflow, exceed abundantly) that of the Pharisees. Not more rules but deeper transformation — righteousness that comes from the heart, not merely from meticulous legal compliance.

hist.context: Vv.13–16 apply the Beatitudes to the community's social function: salt preserving, light illuminating. Both metaphors describe the kingdom community as functioning for the sake of the world, not merely for itself. Vv.17–20 establish the relationship between Jesus and Torah: fulfilment, not abolition. The startling claim of v.20 — surpassing Pharisaic righteousness — sets up the six antitheses that follow by asking: what does a deeper righteousness look like in practice?

refs.note: A light for the Gentiles — the Servant's calling applied to Jesus's community. The "light of the world" metaphor connects the disciples' calling to the Servant's mission.
Do not add to or subtract from what I command you. Jesus's statement about the Law's permanence echoes Deuteronomy's same insistence on Torah's integrity.

notes.note: Calvin on the non-resistance passages: Christ is not establishing civil law or governing society. He is governing the disposition of the Christian's heart. The magistrate must use the sword; the soldier must fight. But in personal relationships the Christian's spirit is to absorb injury rather than retaliate. Institutional and personal are distinct spheres.

Calvin on 'be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect': This perfection is not sinless perfection — none achieve it in this life. It is the perfection of the will's direction: the heart wholly oriented toward God and neighbour rather than self. The standard governs the present as the goal the sanctified life moves toward.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'you have heard... but I say' discusses whether Jesus corrects misunderstanding of the OT or extends its own demands. The antitheses deepen rather than contradict: the OT's external prohibitions always implied internal demands (do not murder implies do not hate). Jesus makes the implication explicit.

The NET note on teleios (perfect/complete) traces its LXX usage for the wholeness/integrity of the covenant person (Gen 6:9 — Noah was blameless/complete). It identifies qualitative completeness — the total orientation of the whole person toward God — not sinless moral perfection.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson identifies the six Antitheses as Jesus' authoritative interpretation of Torah. The authority formula 'but I say to you' (egō de legō hymin) has no parallel in rabbinic literature — rabbis always argued from precedent. Jesus speaks in his own name with the authority of the covenant lawgiver.

Robertson notes 'love your enemies' has no precise OT citation — no text says 'hate your enemy,' though the culture of exclusion had developed this implication from nationalist readings. The command to perfection is the section's summary: the standard is the character of God, not legal minimums.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Chrysostom on turning the other cheek: This is not cowardice — it is the strength of the person who does not need to defend their honour because their honour rests with God, not human opinion. The non-retaliating disciple is free from the tyranny of requiring vindication from other people.

Augustine: Be perfect as your Father in heaven is perfect — not to love only those who love us is the minimum; to love enemies is the maximum. The Father sends rain on just and unjust alike — his love is not conditioned on response. To love this way is to participate in the divine character.

sections.header: Verses 21–48 — The Six Antitheses: You Have Heard... But I Say

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson on the antitheses structure: you have heard... but I say to you. Robertson notes that Jesus does not quote Moses and then agree with him; he quotes the tradition's reading of Moses and then deepens or corrects it. The authority claim is Christological: I say to you has no parallel in rabbinic teaching.

Robertson on love your enemies (v.44): the command to love enemies is uniquely Christian and uniquely demanding. Robertson traces the Greek agapate (love with agape, covenant love) as distinct from the natural affection that even pagans show to friends. The imitation-of-the-Father ground (v.45) is the command's theological foundation.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The Catena records Chrysostom's treatment of the first antithesis (murder/anger): the law prohibited the act; Jesus prohibits the inner state that produces it. Chrysostom: anger is the seed of murder; by addressing the root, Christ heals what the law could only punish at the fruit.

The Catena traces the patristic development of love your enemies as the summary of Christian ethics. Chrysostom: love for enemies is the distinguishing mark of the Christian community -- any group loves its friends. The imitation of the Father (v.45) makes enemy-love a participation in divine perfection.

Calvin on the antitheses: Christ is not abolishing the law but exposing the Pharisees' superficial reading of it. The law always required the inner disposition; the tradition had reduced it to external compliance. Christ restores the law's full demand.

Calvin on be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect (v.48): this is not a command to sinless perfection but to the completeness of covenant love that the Father shows. The Father's perfection is his comprehensive love -- love extended even to enemies. That completeness is the standard.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the antithesis formula (you have heard... but I say) discusses whether Jesus is contrasting his teaching with Moses or with the Pharisaic interpretation of Moses. The note traces the scholarly debate and concludes that Jesus is deepening and radicalising the law, not replacing it.

The NET note on be perfect (teleios, v.48) discusses the semantic range: complete, mature, whole, fully developed. The note traces the parallel in Luke 6:36 (be merciful) and identifies the Matthean perfection as covenant completeness -- the love that holds nothing back.

heb.paragraph: Six times Jesus uses this formula, setting his authority alongside — or above — Moses. No rabbi said 'I say to you' as a counter to Torah. The emphatic pronoun ἐγώ is unnecessary grammatically but theologically explosive: Jesus claims personal authority to reinterpret the Law of God.

'Be perfect as your heavenly Father is perfect' (v.48). Teleios does not mean flawless but complete, whole, lacking nothing in maturity. The standard is not sinless perfection but wholehearted love — love that includes enemies, just as the Father's rain falls on all.

hist.context: The six antitheses (vv.21–48) are the demonstration of what "surpassing righteousness" (v.20) and "fulfilment" (v.17) look like in practice. Each follows the pattern: "You have heard... but I say." The pairs are: murder/anger, adultery/lust, divorce, oaths, retaliation/non-resistance, neighbour love/enemy love. The progression moves from the visible act to the invisible intention, and from the management of harm to the active creation of good.

refs.note: Do not hate your brother in your heart... love your neighbour. Jesus's antithesis on anger extends Leviticus's own inward dimension of the command.
Be holy because I, the LORD your God, am holy. The command to be perfect as God is perfect (v.48) echoes the Holiness Code's foundational imperative.

ppl.name: Jesus
ppl.role: New Moses; teacher on the mountain
ppl.text: Teaches with authority (7:29) — his own authority, not citing tradition. The Sermon is simultaneously Torah and its fulfilment.

ppl.name: The disciples
ppl.text: Seated at his feet on the mountain — the posture of Torah students before their rabbi.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them."NotePlēroō — the same word used for OT fulfilment throughout Matthew. Jesus does not set aside Torah; he brings it to its complete intended meaning, driving to the heart-level intention behind every commandment.

src.title: The Sermon on the Mount
src.quote: Chs.5–7 are Matthew's first great discourse — the new Torah given on a mountain by the new Moses. The five discourses of Matthew (chs.5–7, 10, 13, 18, 23–25) mirror the five books of Moses.
src.note: The structural parallel to the Pentateuch is deliberate.

src.title: Antithesis structure
src.quote: You have heard... but I say. This formula was used by rabbis to contrast opinions. Jesus uses his own authority ("I say") rather than citing tradition ("Rabbi X says"), which explains the crowds' astonishment in 7:29.
src.note: The authority claim is the most radical feature of the antitheses.

rec.text: The most commented-upon passage in the NT. Augustine's "Our Lord's Sermon on the Mount" (c.AD 395) established the pattern of reading it as a moral charter. Luther read it as a mirror to show sin; Bonhoeffer (Cost of Discipleship) read it as a literal call.
The Beatitudes are saturated with Psalm language — Ps 37 (meek inherit the earth), Ps 24:3–6 (pure in heart see God), Ps 51 (mourning). The Sermon is a new Psalter for the new covenant community.

rows.label: The Beatitudes: Kingdom Character in Eight Portraits
rows.text: Blessed are the poor in spirit... the mourning... the meek. The eight Beatitudes are not eight commandments but eight character portraits. They describe what kingdom citizens look like, not what they must achieve.

rows.label: The Beatitudes: Kingdom Character in Eight Portraits
rows.text: Blessed are the poor in spirit... the mourning... the meek. The eight Beatitudes are not eight commandments but eight character portraits. They describe what kingdom citizens look like, not what they must achieve.

rows.label: The Antitheses: The Law's Full Demand — 5:21-48
rows.text: You have heard... but I say: six times Jesus moves from external prohibition to the internal reality it implies. The standard is not legal minimalism but the character of God himself: be perfect as your Father is perfect.

tx.title: 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth' (Ps 37:11)
tx.content: MT Ps 37:11: vaanivim yirshu eretz. Exact LXX quotation in Matthew 5:5.
tx.note: The Ps 37 context contrasts the wicked who flourish temporarily with the meek who wait for YHWH and will inherit the land permanently. Jesus quotes the psalm's conclusion as a Beatitude — covenant land promise becomes eschatological new creation promise for kingdom citizens.

debate.title: The Antitheses: Contradiction or Deepening of Torah?

positions.name: Deepening and completing — not contradicting
positions.argument: Jesus brings out the fullest meaning of the OT. The six antitheses uncover internal demands that external commands always implied.

positions.name: Some antitheses represent genuine modification
positions.argument: The divorce and oath antitheses represent genuine modifications of Mosaic permission (Deut 24:1-4; Num 30) rather than mere deepening.

themes.note: Matthew 5 opens the Sermon on the Mount with the Beatitudes' comprehensive character description and the Antitheses' comprehensive demand. The governing principle: fulfilment means the law's full internal demand, not its external minimum. The standard is the character of God himself.

coaching.tip: The Beatitudes invert expectations: blessed are the poor, mourning, meek. Kingdom values contradict worldly success metrics. These aren't entrance requirements but descriptions of kingdom citizens.

'Unless your righteousness surpasses that of the Pharisees' (v. 20). Not less law but more—heart transformation, not external compliance. The antitheses ('You have heard... but I tell you') radicalize Torah, not abolish it.
